title: Vegetable samosa
subtitle: Spiced potato and pea filling in crisp pastry
excerpt: Crisp triangular pastry cases filled with a dry spiced mixture of potato, peas, and cumin. The filling is deliberately dry (no sauce) so the pastry stays crisp after frying. The dough is made from plain flour and oil rather than butter, giving a shattery, brittle crust that is distinct from puff or shortcrust pastry. Served as a starter with chutneys.
sourceNotes: Samosa is a triangular pastry snack documented across the Middle East and South Asia from the medieval period onward. The South Asian version, with spiced potato filling, has been a staple street food and restaurant starter on the British Indian menu since the 1960s. The pastry dough is deliberately unleavened and oil-enriched for the characteristic crisp texture.

content.text: The samosa dough is oil-enriched and unleavened. It rolls thin and holds a fold without tearing, but needs to rest for at least 30 minutes after mixing to relax the gluten, otherwise it springs back when rolled and the pastry is thick and tough rather than thin and crisp. Rest before rolling.

The filling must be completely dry. Any moisture from the potato or peas will steam inside the sealed pastry and soften it from the inside. Cook the filling until all visible moisture has evaporated; spread it on a plate and let it cool before filling. If the filling is warm when it goes inside, it will generate steam during frying and the pastry will blister and separate from the filling.

items.name: Vegetable oil
items.prepNote: for the dough, plus 1 litre for frying

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: peeled and cut into small cubes

content.text: Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{vegetable-oil}} (3 tbsp) together until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time (about 5 to 6 tablespoons total) until a firm dough forms. Knead for 2 minutes, then wrap and rest for 30 minutes. Meanwhile, boil the {{potato}} in salted water for 8 minutes until just tender. Drain and cool. Heat 1 tablespoon of oil in a pan over medium heat. Add the {{cumin-seeds}} and {{mustard-seeds-black}} and cook for 20 seconds. Add the {{ginger-root}}, {{chilli-green}}, and {{turmeric}} and stir for 30 seconds. Add the potatoes and {{peas-frozen}} and cook for 3 minutes, stirring, until dry. Stir in the {{garam-masala}}, {{coriander}}, and lemon juice. Season and cool completely.

Divide the rested dough into 4 pieces. Roll each piece into a thin oval (about 3 mm thick). Cut each oval in half. Moisten the straight edge of one half with water and form a cone by bringing the two straight-edge corners together, pressing to seal. Fill the cone two-thirds full with the cooled filling. Moisten the open edge and press firmly to seal, forming a triangle. Repeat for the remaining pieces. Heat 1 litre of {{vegetable-oil}} to 180°C. Fry the samosas in batches of 2 to 3 for 5 to 6 minutes, turning once, until golden and crisp. Drain on paper.

items.fix: Press the seam firmly with dampened fingers and pinch the edges. The filling must be completely dry, any moisture creates steam that forces the seam open
items.cause: Seal not firm enough or filling too wet

items.fix: Rest the dough for the full 30 minutes. Roll to about 3 mm. It should be thin enough to see light through
items.cause: Dough not rested or not rolled thin enough

items.fix: Fry at 180°C. Check with a thermometer or test with a small piece of dough. It should sizzle immediately and rise
items.symptom: Pastry pale and oily rather than crisp and golden

items.fix: Fill the cone two-thirds full only. The filling expands slightly during frying

content.text: The vegetable samosa is the standard vegetarian starter at British Indian restaurants. It sits alongside onion bhaji as the starter pair of the classic curry-house meal. Making samosas at home takes more work than most curry-house dishes. The folding and sealing want some patience, but the result is much fresher than shop-bought. The freeze-and-fry-from-frozen method means the work is not needed on the day you serve them.
